title: The Winter Peak Gap: Why a Booked-Out July Doesn't Guarantee a Better Year for Tourism Operators
linkedin:
A fully booked July calendar and a stronger bank balance in August aren't the same thing. For tourism and accommodation operators, they're often not even correlated the way owners expect.

We see this pattern every winter. Queensland operators fill rooms as families escape the cold. Alpine lodges across NSW and Victoria run close to capacity through ski season. The occupancy numbers look brilliant.

Here's the mechanism most owners miss. Running a business at full capacity for a short burst costs more per dollar of revenue than running it steadily for longer. Casual staff come on at premium rates because the roster has to expand fast. Linen, provisioning and maintenance get booked at whatever price is available, not the planned-ahead rate.

Then the peak ends. Rent, loan repayments, insurance and the core wage bill keep running at the same level through the quiet weeks either side, whether or not the cash from the peak was set aside to cover them.

The operators who come out of a strong season in a stronger cash position are the ones who mapped the whole season's cash flow before the first booking landed, not after the last guest checked out. A rolling 13-week view turns a good July into protected reserves for the shoulder months, instead of a number that looked strong on the booking sheet and thinner by the time it hit the bank.

A strong peak is a genuine asset. What happens to the cash it generates is a decision, not an accident.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#AustralianBusiness #CashFlow #BrisbaneSME
facebook:
Ever noticed a booked-out winter season doesn't always translate into a bigger bank balance a few months later?

It's not bad luck. Running at full capacity for a short burst, extra casual staff at premium rates, linen and provisioning at short notice, costs more per dollar of revenue than trading steadily for longer. Then the quiet weeks either side still have rent, loan repayments and wages to cover, whether or not any of the peak's cash was set aside for them.

If you run a seasonal tourism or accommodation business, has a strong peak ever left you tighter than expected a few months on?

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for tourism and accommodation operators along the East Coast: if winter has been your busiest trading stretch this year, whether that is a Queensland property filling up with families escaping the cold or an alpine lodge running close to capacity through ski season, it is worth checking that the cash from that peak is actually being protected.

A short, intense trading window costs more to run than the same revenue spread evenly across a longer period. Casual staff come on at premium rates to cover the extra demand, and stock, cleaning and maintenance often get booked at whatever price is available rather than a planned-ahead rate. None of that shows up as a problem on any single booking. It shows up later, when rent, loan repayments and the core wage bill keep running through the quieter weeks either side of the peak, regardless of whether the peak cash was set aside to cover them.

The businesses that come out of a strong season stronger are usually the ones that mapped the whole season cash flow before it started, not after the calendar emptied out. If a busy few weeks this winter have left you wondering why the bank account does not quite match the bookings, that gap is worth understanding properly.
